critique.summary: The article discusses an experiment with using automated verification methods to trust AI-generated code without manual review. The author proposes verifying code correctness through property-based testing, mutation testing, and other constraints, suggesting this approach could reduce the need for human review in production settings.
mainPoints: The author differentiates between reviewing AI-generated code line-by-line and verifying it through automated checks.
Verification involves property-based testing, mutation testing, ensuring no side effects, and type-checking/linting.
The author believes these methods can sufficiently constrain the code to trust it without manual reading.
Maintainability and readability may be less relevant if code is treated like compiled binaries.
The approach currently has overhead but could improve as tooling and AI agents advance.
Related work includes formal verification, large unit test suites, and projects aiming for no human code review.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes established software testing concepts such as property-based testing and mutation testing, and correctly applies them to the context of verifying AI-generated code. The references to existing tools like Hypothesis and mutmut are factual and well-known in software engineering. The claims about reducing the need for manual review through these methods are plausible but represent an experimental approach rather than a widely adopted standard. The discussion of related work and challenges is consistent with current research trends. Overall, the article provides a truthful and reasoned perspective based on the author's experimentation and existing literature.
considerations: The article does not address potential limitations of automated verification in detecting logical errors or security vulnerabilities that tests might miss.
It does not discuss the risks of relying solely on tests without human insight, especially for complex or safety-critical systems.
The overhead and complexity of setting up comprehensive property-based and mutation tests may be prohibitive for many projects.
The article assumes the correctness of the test suite itself, which is a critical dependency that can be challenging to guarantee.
There is no mention of how this approach scales to larger, more complex codebases beyond the simplified FizzBuzz example.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion critiques the use of FizzBuzz as a benchmark for verifying AI-generated code, emphasizing the complexity of real-world software testing and the limitations of AI in fully automating code review and verification. Commenters highlight the importance of human oversight, the challenges of testing beyond correctness, and the role of formal specifications and advanced testing methods.

comments.author: jryio
comments.text:
This is a naïve approach, not just because it uses FizzBuzz, but because it ignores the fundamental complexity of software as a system of abstractions. Testing often involves understanding these abstractions and testing for/against them.

For those of us with decades of experience and who use coding agents for hours per-day, we learned that even with extended context engineering these models are not magically covering the testing space more than 50%.

If you asked your coding agent to develop a memory allocator, it would not also 'automatically verify' the memory allocator against all failure modes. It is your responsibility as an engineer to have long-term learning and regular contact with the world to inform the testing approach.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view on the complexity of software testing and the limitations of AI in covering all failure modes, emphasizing the engineer's responsibility.

comments.author: brianmcnulty
comments.text:
I think generative AI works best as a code review assistant rather than something that should be relied entirely upon for code review. LLMs can provide helpful advice and help engineers think of something they might not have thought about when writing, but they cannot review code in-depth the way we can. There are plenty of blatantly obvious security issues (usually on code generated by an LLM in large patches) I've seen pass LLM and automated code review before.

LLM code review (with the right tools and setup) can encourage engineers to write more pragmatic code, help write more in-depth tests, and submit overall better code without having to involve someone else, but a language model can't replace an actual human code review for production systems. We also should not be thinking of code as "correct" or "incorrect" because that doesn't measure code quality and security. Additionally, code can be classified as correct through tests but still have major security issues not covered by a test or be something that utilizes too much CPU/memory in production.
comments.analysis: Accurately assesses the complementary role of AI in code review while underscoring the necessity of human expertise for security and quality assurance.

comments.author: phailhaus
comments.text: Using FizzBuzz as your proxy for "unreviewed code" is extremely misleading. It has practically no complexity, it's completely self-contained and easy to verify. In any codebase of even modest complexity, the challenge shifts from "does this produce the correct outputs" to "is this going to let me grow the way I need it to in the future" and thornier questions like "does this have the performance characteristics that I need".
comments.analysis: Correctly points out that FizzBuzz is an inadequate benchmark for complex software verification, highlighting broader challenges in maintainability and performance.

comments.author: tedivm
comments.text:
While I understand why people want to skip code reviews, I think it is an absolute mistake at this point in time. I think AI coding assistants are great, but I've seen them fail or go down the wrong path enough times (even with things like spec driven development) where I don't think it's reasonable to not review code. Everything from development paths in production code, improper implementations, security risks: all of those are just as likely to happen with an AI as a Human, and any team that let's humans push to production without a review would absolutely be ridiculed for it.

Again, I'm not opposed to AI coding. I know a lot of people are. I have multiple open source projects that were 100% created with AI assistants, and wrote a blog post about it you can see in my post history. I'm not anti-ai, but I do think that developers have some responsibility for the code they create with those tools.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced perspective on AI-assisted development, cautioning against skipping human code reviews due to risks and past AI failures.

comments.author: pron
comments.text:
> The code must pass property-based tests

Who writes the tests? It can be ok to trust code that passes tests if you can trust the tests.

There are, however, other problems. I frequently see agents write code that's functionally correct but that they won't be able to evolve for long. That's also what happened with Anthropic's attempt to have agents write a C compiler. They had thousands of human-written tests, but at some point the agents couldn't get the software to converge. Fixing a bug created another.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the trustworthiness of tests themselves and the difficulty in evolving AI-generated codebases.

comments.author: ventana
comments.text:
I might be missing the point of the article, but from what I understand, the TL;DR is, "cover your code with tests", be it unit tests, functional tests, or mutants.

Each of these approaches is just fine and widely used, and none of them can be called "automated verification", which, if my understanding of the term is correct, is more about mathematical proof that the program works as expected.

The article mostly talks about automatic test generation.
comments.analysis: Correctly distinguishes between automated test generation and formal automated verification, clarifying the article's scope.

comments.author: jghn
comments.text: I do think that GenAI will lead to a rise in mutation testing, property testing, and fuzzing. But it's worth people keeping in mind that there are reasons why these aren't already ubiquitous. Among other issues, they can be computationally expensive, especially mutation testing.
comments.analysis: Insightfully predicts increased adoption of advanced testing methods due to AI, while noting practical limitations like computational cost.

comments.author: Ancalagon
comments.text: Even with mutation testing doesn’t this still require review of the testing code?
comments.analysis: Raises an important point about the necessity of reviewing test code itself, highlighting a potential blind spot in automated testing.

comments.author: sharkjacobs
comments.text: I'm having a hard time wrapping my head around how this can scale beyond trivial programs like simplified FizzBuzz.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common skepticism about scaling verification methods beyond simple examples, reflecting practical concerns.

comments.author: otabdeveloper4
comments.text:
This one is pretty easy!

Just write your business requirements in a clear, unambiguous and exhaustive manner using a formal specification language.

Bam, no coding required.
comments.analysis: Suggests a well-established approach to reduce coding errors, though it may underestimate the difficulty of creating exhaustive formal specifications.

comments.author: duskdozer
comments.text: So are we finally past the stage where people pretend they're actually reading any of the code their LLMs are dumping out?
comments.analysis: Appears cynical and somewhat dismissive, implying users blindly trust AI-generated code without verification.

comments.author: andai
comments.analysis: Provides an uninformative and incomplete comment that adds no meaningful insight to the discussion.
